title: Inside the Transformer
subtitle: Q, K, V and the machine that pays attention to everything at once
theme: Open the box: build deep, intuitive AND technical understanding of how a Transformer actually works.
learningObjectives: Explain self-attention using the Query/Key/Value (Q/K/V) intuition with a real-world analogy
Walk through scaled dot-product attention step by step, including why we divide by the square root of the dimension
Describe multi-head attention and why multiple 'spotlights' beat one
Understand positional encoding and why a parallel model needs order injected back in
Trace the full encoder-decoder stack, including residual connections, layer norm, and feed-forward layers
Compute a tiny attention example by hand to make the math concrete and unscary
storyHook: Yesterday we watched eight researchers fire the RNN and bet everything on attention. Today we walk into the engine room and see the bet up close. There is one mechanism beating at the heart of every model you use — and at first glance its formula looks like intimidating mathematics. By the end of this session you will compute it by hand with numbers small enough to do in your head, and the most famous diagram in modern AI will stop being a black box and start feeling obvious.

slides.title: The Map: What's Inside a Transformer
slides.body:
## A tour of the engine room

Yesterday we learned *why* the Transformer exists. Today we learn *how* it works — and we're going to do it without fear of the math.

Here is the whole machine in one picture. Don't panic; we'll take it apart piece by piece.

```
        INPUT TEXT
           │
   [ token embeddings + positional encoding ]
           │
   ┌───────▼────────┐         ┌────────────────┐
   │   ENCODER x N   │────────▶│   DECODER x N   │──▶ OUTPUT
   └────────────────┘         └────────────────┘

 Each ENCODER block:           Each DECODER block:
  • Multi-Head Self-Attention   • Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention
  • Add & LayerNorm             • Add & LayerNorm
  • Feed-Forward Network        • Encoder-Decoder Attention
  • Add & LayerNorm             • Add & LayerNorm
                                • Feed-Forward Network
                                • Add & LayerNorm
```

### The five ideas we'll master today

1. **Self-attention (Q/K/V)** — how every word looks at every other word.
2. **Scaled dot-product attention** — the exact recipe, with a hand-worked example.
3. **Multi-head attention** — why we run several attention 'spotlights' in parallel.
4. **Positional encoding** — how a parallel model knows word order.
5. **The stack** — residual connections, layer norm, and feed-forward layers that glue it together.

> Promise for today: you'll leave able to *explain the diagram above to a colleague* and *do the core math on a napkin.*
slides.speakerNotes: Reassure the room immediately — the big diagram looks scary but we'll dismantle it brick by brick. Tell them the goal isn't to memorize the picture, it's to understand the STORY of how information flows through it. Ask: 'How many of you have seen this diagram before and felt lost?' Normalize that. Promise the napkin-math moment — people remember a session where they did the math themselves.
slides.marketConnection: This exact block diagram, with minor tweaks, describes GPT, Gemini, Claude, and Llama in 2026. Understanding it once means understanding the architecture under the entire industry.

slides.title: Self-Attention: Every Word Asks Every Other Word a Question
slides.body:
## The core idea before any math

The single most important concept in the Transformer is **self-attention**. Here's the plain-English version.

Take the sentence: **'The animal didn't cross the street because *it* was too tired.'**

What does 'it' refer to — the animal or the street? You know instantly: the animal. To understand the word 'it,' your brain *looked at the other words* and decided which ones were relevant. **That is self-attention.** Every word builds its meaning by looking at the other words in the sentence and weighting how much each one matters.

### The genius reframing: a tiny search engine per word

For each word, the Transformer creates **three different versions** of it by multiplying its embedding by three learned weight matrices:

- **Query (Q):** 'Here's what I'm looking for.' (The question this word is asking.)
- **Key (K):** 'Here's what I contain / what I'm about.' (How this word advertises itself.)
- **Value (V):** 'Here's the actual information I'll hand over if you pick me.'

### The library analogy (use this forever)

Imagine you walk into a library with a **question** (your Query). Every book has a **label on its spine** (its Key). You compare your question against every spine and find the best matches. Then you pull those books and read their **contents** (their Values), weighted by how good a match each was.

```
Word 'it' →  Query: "what noun am I standing in for?"
Word 'animal' → Key: "I am a subject noun"  ✓ strong match → high weight
Word 'street' → Key: "I am an object noun"  ~ weak match  → low weight
→ 'it' pulls mostly the VALUE of 'animal'
```

That's it. Self-attention is just **every word querying every word's keys to decide whose values to blend in.** Next slide: the exact recipe — and it's only four steps.
slides.speakerNotes: The 'it' = animal example is the most famous self-attention example in the field for a reason — run it live and make them feel their own brain doing attention. Then the library analogy is your workhorse for the whole day: Query = your question, Key = the label on the book spine, Value = the book's actual contents. Ask: 'When you search a library, do you read every book fully, or do you check spines first and then read the best matches?' That's exactly Q-K matching then V retrieval.
slides.marketConnection: When 2026 interpretability tools show you 'attention heatmaps' over a document, they are literally visualizing these Q-K match weights — which words each word decided to look at.

slides.title: Scaled Dot-Product Attention: The Recipe
slides.body:
## The one formula at the heart of it all

Here is the famous equation. We'll demystify every symbol.

```
Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax( (Q · K^T) / sqrt(d_k) ) · V
```

It looks scary. It is actually **four friendly steps**:

### Step 1 — Score: how well does each Query match each Key?
Take the **dot product** of a word's Query with every word's Key. A dot product is just a similarity score — bigger means 'more aligned.' This gives a grid of raw scores (every word vs. every word).

### Step 2 — Scale: divide by sqrt(d_k)
We divide every score by the square root of the key dimension `d_k`. **Why?** When vectors are long (large `d_k`), dot products get large, which pushes softmax into extreme, 'spiky' regions where gradients vanish and learning stalls. Dividing by `sqrt(d_k)` keeps the scores in a sane range. It's a thermostat that stops the numbers from exploding.

### Step 3 — Softmax: turn scores into weights that sum to 1
**Softmax** squashes the scores into positive weights that add up to 1 — a probability distribution of attention. High scores become big slices of attention; low scores become slivers.

### Step 4 — Weighted sum of Values
Multiply each word's **Value** by its attention weight and add them up. The result is a new, context-aware representation of the word — a blend of all the words it cared about.

```
      scores → scale → softmax(weights) → blend the Values
  "how much   "keep    "make them      "mix the info
   does each   numbers   sum to 1"       accordingly"
   word match"  calm"
```

> The whole formula in one sentence: **Score every word against every word, calm the numbers, turn them into focus weights, then blend everyone's information by those weights.** Next slide: we do it with actual numbers.
slides.speakerNotes: Read the formula aloud once, then immediately say 'that's just four steps' and walk them. The sqrt(d_k) is the one bit people never understand — use the 'thermostat / keep the numbers calm' framing so softmax doesn't go spiky. Emphasize softmax = 'forces a focus budget that sums to 100%.' Ask the class: 'If one score is huge and the rest tiny, what will softmax do?' (Answer: nearly all attention on that one word — a sharp spotlight.)
slides.marketConnection: This four-step recipe runs billions of times per second across the GPU fleets powering every 2026 chatbot. Optimizing it (e.g., FlashAttention) is a major reason inference got cheaper year over year.

slides.title: Attention by Hand: A Tiny Worked Example
slides.body:
## Let's actually compute it (numbers small enough for a napkin)

We'll do attention for a 3-word 'sentence': **A, B, C**. We'll use 2-dimensional vectors so we can do it by hand. Suppose we already have the Q, K, V vectors:

```
Word   Query(Q)    Key(K)      Value(V)
 A     [1, 0]      [1, 0]      [10, 0]
 B     [0, 1]      [0, 1]      [0, 10]
 C     [1, 1]      [1, 1]      [5,  5]
```

**Goal:** compute the new representation of word **A**.

### Step 1 — Scores (Q_A dot each Key)
```
Q_A · K_A = (1*1 + 0*0) = 1
Q_A · K_B = (1*0 + 0*1) = 0
Q_A · K_C = (1*1 + 0*1) = 1
raw scores for A:  [1, 0, 1]
```

### Step 2 — Scale by sqrt(d_k), d_k = 2
```
sqrt(2) ≈ 1.414
scaled: [1/1.414, 0/1.414, 1/1.414] = [0.707, 0, 0.707]
```

### Step 3 — Softmax (turn into weights summing to 1)
```
e^0.707 ≈ 2.028
e^0     = 1.000
e^0.707 ≈ 2.028
sum = 5.056
weights ≈ [0.401, 0.198, 0.401]   ← sums to 1.0 ✓
```
So word A pays ~40% attention to A, ~20% to B, ~40% to C.

### Step 4 — Blend the Values
```
new_A = 0.401*[10,0] + 0.198*[0,10] + 0.401*[5,5]
      = [4.01, 0] + [0, 1.98] + [2.005, 2.005]
      ≈ [6.02, 3.99]
```

**Result: the new representation of A is about [6.0, 4.0].** Notice it's no longer just A's original value [10,0] — it has absorbed information from B and C, weighted by relevance. That's a context-aware word.

> You just did self-attention by hand. Everything else is this, repeated for every word, in higher dimensions, very fast.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the centerpiece of the whole day — slow down and let them compute alongside you. Hand out the numbers and walk each step on the board. The 'aha' is Step 4: A's new vector [6,4] is a BLEND — it has soaked up B and C. Pause there: 'Word A now literally carries traces of B and C inside it. That's how context gets baked in.' Ask them to predict: 'If we computed word B, which value would dominate its blend?' (B's own, since Q_B matches K_B most.)
slides.marketConnection: This is not a toy detached from reality — it is exactly the operation, scaled to thousands of dimensions and millions of tokens, that lets a 2026 model 'understand' a 300-page document you paste into it.

slides.title: Multi-Head Attention: Many Spotlights at Once
slides.body:
## Why one attention is not enough

A single attention mechanism gives you *one* way of relating words. But language has *many* kinds of relationships happening simultaneously:

- **Grammar:** which noun does this verb agree with?
- **Reference:** what does 'it' point to?
- **Meaning/topic:** which words are about the same idea?
- **Position:** which words are nearby?

Forcing all of these through a single attention is like asking one person to simultaneously watch for grammar, meaning, and reference. They'll do all of them poorly.

### The fix: run several attentions in parallel — 'heads'

**Multi-head attention** runs several independent attention mechanisms (say, 8 or 16 'heads') *at the same time*. Each head gets its own learned Q/K/V matrices, so each one learns to focus on a *different kind* of relationship. Then their outputs are concatenated and mixed back together.

```
          ┌─ Head 1 (maybe tracks subject-verb) ─┐
  input → ├─ Head 2 (maybe tracks 'it' refs)     ─┤ → concat → mix → output
          ├─ Head 3 (maybe tracks adjacent words)─┤
          └─ Head 8 (maybe tracks topic)         ─┘
```

### The panel-of-experts analogy

Instead of one generalist reading the sentence, imagine a **panel of eight specialists**, each reading the same sentence through a different lens, then pooling their notes. The result is a far richer understanding than any single reader could produce.

### A crucial detail

Each head works in a *smaller* dimension (the total dimension is split across heads), so multi-head attention costs roughly the same as single-head — you get diversity nearly for free. This is one of the quiet elegances of the design.

> Self-attention lets words look at each other. Multi-head attention lets them look at each other in *many different ways at once.*
slides.speakerNotes: Lead with the problem: one attention = one type of relationship, but language has many at once. The 'panel of eight specialists pooling notes' analogy lands well. Be honest that we can't perfectly label what each head does — but researchers HAVE found heads that track specific things (subject-verb, coreference). Ask: 'If you were reading a contract, what different 'lenses' would you want to read it through at the same time?' (legal terms, dates, parties, obligations) — that's why multiple heads help.
slides.marketConnection: Frontier 2026 models use dozens of attention heads per layer across dozens of layers. A variant called 'grouped-query attention' (sharing keys/values across heads) is a key 2024-2026 efficiency trick that makes serving these models cheaper.

slides.title: Positional Encoding: Teaching a Parallel Machine About Order
slides.body:
## The price of going parallel

Here's a subtle problem we created yesterday. By processing all words **at once** instead of one-by-one, the Transformer threw away the natural sense of order that RNNs got for free.

To raw self-attention, the sentence 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' look **identical** — same set of words, and attention is order-blind. That's a disaster for language, where order *is* meaning.

### The fix: stamp each word with its position

**Positional encoding** adds a unique 'position signature' vector to each word's embedding *before* attention sees it. Word 1 gets one signature, word 2 another, and so on. Now the model can tell where each word sits.

### How the original paper did it: sine waves

The 2017 paper used a clever trick: a set of **sine and cosine waves** of different frequencies. Each position gets a unique combination of wave values, like a fingerprint.

```
pos 0: [sin/cos at many frequencies] → unique pattern
pos 1: [slightly shifted pattern]    → unique pattern
pos 2: [...]                         → unique pattern
```

Why waves? Because they give the model a smooth, continuous sense of *relative* distance — the difference between position 5 and position 8 looks consistent no matter where in the sentence you are. It also generalizes to longer sequences than seen in training.

### The clock analogy

Think of an analog clock: the hour, minute, and second hands together encode a unique time using overlapping cycles. Positional encoding does the same with overlapping waves to encode 'where am I in the sentence.'

### Modern note (2026)

The field largely moved to **rotary positional embeddings (RoPE)** and similar relative schemes, which handle very long contexts more gracefully. But the core idea is unchanged: **inject order, because parallel attention has none.**
slides.speakerNotes: The key 'gotcha' to surface: going parallel cost us order, so we have to bolt it back on. Use 'dog bites man' vs 'man bites dog' — without positional encoding they're identical to the model, which is obviously wrong. Don't get bogged down in the sine math; the clock analogy (overlapping cycles encode a unique time) is enough. Mention RoPE as the modern upgrade so they're not surprised when they see it. Ask: 'Why can't we just process in order like before?' (Because that kills the parallelism that made Transformers fast.)
slides.marketConnection: RoPE and its long-context extensions are exactly what enables the 1M-2M token context windows marketed heavily in 2026 — positional encoding is the unsung hero behind 'paste your whole codebase' features.

slides.title: Residuals and Layer Norm: The Glue That Makes Deep Possible
slides.body:
## How do you stack dozens of layers without it collapsing?

A Transformer isn't one attention block — it's **many stacked** (the original had 6 encoder + 6 decoder; modern models have dozens to over a hundred). Stacking deep networks is dangerous: signals degrade and gradients vanish. Two simple mechanisms make depth safe.

### 1. Residual (skip) connections — 'Add'

Around every sub-layer, the Transformer adds the **input back to the output**:

```
output = LayerNorm( x + SubLayer(x) )
                    ▲
             the original x is added back
```

Why? It gives information a **highway** to skip past each block unchanged. If a layer has nothing useful to add, the original signal still flows through untouched. This is what lets gradients reach the bottom layers even in a 100-layer network — it's the same idea (from ResNets) that first let vision models go truly deep.

**Analogy:** it's like keeping the original draft alongside every edit, so you never lose the source even after fifty revisions.

### 2. Layer Normalization — 'Norm'

After adding, we **normalize**: re-center and re-scale the numbers so they stay in a stable, well-behaved range. Without this, values can drift huge or tiny as they pass through layers, and training becomes unstable.

**Analogy:** it's an automatic audio leveler that keeps every layer's 'volume' consistent so the next layer always gets a clean, predictable signal.

### Together: 'Add & Norm'

Every sub-layer in the diagram is wrapped in **Add & Norm**. It's unglamorous plumbing, but without it, deep Transformers simply would not train. These two tricks are why we can scale to the giant depths that make modern models smart.

> Attention is the star. Residuals and layer norm are the stage crew that let the show run for 100 acts.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame this as 'the boring plumbing that secretly makes everything possible.' The residual = 'highway / keep the original draft alongside every edit' analogy is the one to drill. LayerNorm = 'audio leveler keeping volume consistent.' Stress that without these, you literally cannot stack deep — and depth is where the intelligence comes from. Ask: 'Why might adding the original input back to a layer's output protect against a bad layer ruining everything?' (The signal can route around it.)
slides.marketConnection: Residual connections and normalization are so fundamental that every 2026 model uses them; recent variants (RMSNorm, pre-norm placement) are small tweaks that improved training stability at the massive scales used today.

slides.title: The Feed-Forward Layer: Where Words Think Privately
slides.body:
## Attention mixes; feed-forward digests

After attention lets each word gather context from its neighbors, every word passes — *independently* — through a small **feed-forward network (FFN)**. This is a two-layer neural network applied to each position separately and identically.

```
FFN(x) = Linear2( activation( Linear1(x) ) )
```

Typically it **expands** the dimension wide (often 4x), applies a non-linear activation (ReLU in the original, GELU/SwiGLU in modern models), then **projects back down**.

### The division of labor (this is the key insight)

- **Attention** = the *social* step. Words talk to each other and gather relevant context. ('What's around me that matters?')
- **Feed-forward** = the *private thinking* step. Each word, now enriched with context, processes that information on its own. ('Given what I've gathered, what does it all mean?')

Think of a meeting: first everyone **shares information** (attention), then each person goes back to their desk and **works through the implications alone** (feed-forward). A Transformer layer is one round of share-then-digest, and stacking layers repeats it many times — gather context, think; gather more context, think more.

### Why it matters more than it looks

The FFN holds a surprisingly large share of a model's parameters — it's where a lot of the model's **stored knowledge** is believed to live. Recent research treats FFN layers as a kind of key-value *memory* of facts learned during training. When a model 'knows' that Paris is the capital of France, that knowledge is largely sitting in feed-forward weights.

> One Transformer layer = **gather context (attention) + think it through (feed-forward)**, wrapped in Add & Norm. Repeat N times. That's the whole machine.
slides.speakerNotes: This slide finally makes the layer feel complete. The meeting analogy is the anchor: attention = everyone shares info; feed-forward = each person goes to their desk and works it out alone. Emphasize the surprising fact that a LOT of the model's factual knowledge lives in these FFN weights, not in attention. Ask: 'After you gather information from colleagues, do you instantly understand it, or do you need quiet time to process? The model needs that too.' That's the FFN.
slides.marketConnection: The 'knowledge lives in feed-forward layers' finding underpins 2026 model-editing and 'unlearning' techniques — startups selling the ability to surgically update or remove a fact from a model often target exactly these weights. It's also why 'Mixture-of-Experts' (many specialized FFNs) is the dominant scaling trick in 2026 frontier models.

slides.title: Encoder vs. Decoder: Two Jobs, One Architecture
slides.body:
## Putting the halves together

The original Transformer had **two towers**: an encoder and a decoder. Understanding their split is the key to understanding the whole model family.

### The Encoder — the 'reader'
The encoder's job is to **understand** the input. Each encoder block does:
1. Multi-head **self-attention** (every input word looks at every other input word — both directions allowed).
2. Add & Norm.
3. Feed-forward.
4. Add & Norm.

It outputs a rich, context-aware representation of the *entire input*. The encoder sees everything at once — it's bidirectional.

### The Decoder — the 'writer'
The decoder **generates** the output one token at a time. Each decoder block has *three* sub-layers:
1. **Masked** self-attention — it can only look at words it has *already generated*, never the future (because at generation time, the future doesn't exist yet). We'll cover masking on Day 3.
2. **Encoder-decoder (cross) attention** — here Queries come from the decoder, but Keys and Values come from the *encoder's* output. This is how the writer 'looks back' at the source — the modern descendant of yesterday's 2015 attention!
3. Feed-forward, each with Add & Norm.

```
ENCODER (reads, bidirectional)      DECODER (writes, left-to-right)
  self-attention                      masked self-attention
  feed-forward          ──────────▶   CROSS-attention (looks at encoder)
                          provides     feed-forward
                          K & V
```

### The three model families (this is the punchline)

- **Encoder-only** (e.g., BERT): great at *understanding* — classification, search, embeddings.
- **Decoder-only** (e.g., GPT, and most 2026 chat models): great at *generating* — the autoregressive 'predict the next token' workhorse.
- **Encoder-decoder** (e.g., T5, original Transformer): great at *transforming* input to output — translation, summarization.

> The 2017 paper gave us all three by splitting one architecture in half. Most of the 2026 generative giants are **decoder-only** — they kept the writer and dropped the separate reader.
slides.speakerNotes: Tie this straight back to Day 1: cross-attention IS the 2015 attention idea, now formalized inside the decoder — the writer looking back at the source. The big payoff is the three families: encoder-only = understand (BERT), decoder-only = generate (GPT/most chatbots), encoder-decoder = transform (translation). Emphasize that today's chat giants are mostly decoder-only. Ask: 'Why must the decoder be forbidden from looking at future words while writing?' (Because when generating, the future words don't exist yet — it would be cheating.)
slides.marketConnection: In June 2026, the chat assistants dominating the market (GPT, Gemini, Claude, Llama, Grok) are overwhelmingly decoder-only. Encoder-only models like BERT quietly power search and retrieval/embedding pipelines behind the scenes — including the 'RAG' systems enterprises use to query their own documents.

slides.title: End-to-End: Watching a Sentence Flow Through
slides.body:
## One complete pass, start to finish

Let's trace a single example all the way through, so every piece clicks into place. Task: translate **'I am happy'** to French.

```
1. TOKENIZE + EMBED
   "I", "am", "happy" → vectors → + positional encoding

2. ENCODER (x N blocks)
   self-attention: "happy" gathers context from "I","am"
   add&norm → feed-forward → add&norm
   OUT: context-rich representation of the whole English sentence

3. DECODER starts generating French, one token at a time:

   step 1: input <start>
      masked self-attn (only sees <start>)
      cross-attn → looks at encoder output, focuses on "I"
      feed-forward → softmax over vocab → picks "Je"

   step 2: input <start> Je
      masked self-attn (sees <start>, Je)
      cross-attn → now focuses on "am"
      → picks "suis"

   step 3: input <start> Je suis
      cross-attn → focuses on "happy"
      → picks "heureux"

   step 4: → picks <end>. Done.

   OUTPUT: "Je suis heureux"
```

### What to notice

- The encoder ran **once**, in parallel, for the whole input.
- The decoder ran **step by step**, each step attending back to the encoder (cross-attention) and to its own past output (masked self-attention).
- At every generation step, the final layer produces a **probability over the whole vocabulary**, and we pick a word (Day 3 covers *how* we pick).
- The attention spotlight visibly **moved** across the source as it generated each French word — exactly the 2015 dream, now built into the architecture.

> This is the entire Transformer in motion. Everything we studied today — Q/K/V, scaling, softmax, multi-head, positional encoding, residuals, layer norm, feed-forward, encoder/decoder — fired in this one trace.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the integration slide — walk it slowly as a single film. The most important beat: the encoder runs ONCE in parallel, but the decoder generates step-by-step, and you can see the cross-attention spotlight march across 'I' → 'am' → 'happy' as it emits 'Je' → 'suis' → 'heureux'. That visual of the moving spotlight ties Day 1 and Day 2 together. Ask: 'At step 2, why can the decoder see Je but not suis yet?' (It hasn't generated suis — masking enforces that.)
slides.marketConnection: This exact generate-one-token-at-a-time loop is why 2026 chatbots 'stream' their answers word by word on your screen — you're watching the decoder's autoregressive loop in real time.

slides.title: Recap + Quick Check
slides.body:
## Lock in the engine room

### The Transformer in seven ideas

1. **Self-attention** — every word looks at every other word to build context.
2. **Q/K/V** — Query (my question), Key (my label), Value (my contents); the library search.
3. **Scaled dot-product attention** — score, scale by sqrt(d_k), softmax, blend values.
4. **Multi-head** — many attention spotlights in parallel, each catching a different relationship.
5. **Positional encoding** — inject word order back into a parallel model.
6. **Add & Norm** — residual highways + normalization make deep stacks trainable.
7. **Feed-forward** — each word thinks privately and stores knowledge.

> One layer = **gather context (attention) → think (feed-forward)**, wrapped in Add & Norm, with positions stamped in. Stack N of them. Split into encoder (read) and decoder (write).

---

### Quick check (discuss in pairs)

**1.** In the library analogy, what do Query, Key, and Value each correspond to?

**2.** Why do we divide attention scores by sqrt(d_k) before softmax?

**3.** Give one reason multi-head attention beats single-head attention.

**4.** A Transformer processes all words at once — so how does it know word order?

**5.** What are the two things a residual connection + layer norm let us do that we otherwise couldn't?

**6.** What's the difference in *what the decoder attends to* in masked self-attention vs. cross-attention?

*(Sketch answers: 1. Query=your question, Key=book spine label, Value=book contents. 2. To keep scores small so softmax doesn't go spiky and kill gradients. 3. Different heads capture different relationship types simultaneously. 4. Positional encoding added to embeddings. 5. Train very deep stacks + keep signal/gradients stable. 6. Masked self-attn looks at the decoder's own past tokens; cross-attn looks at the encoder's output.)*
slides.speakerNotes: Recite the 'one layer = gather then think' mantra together — it's the single most portable summary they can carry. Run the quick check as think-pair-share. Spend extra time on Q2 (sqrt scaling) and Q6 (masked vs cross attention) since those are the most commonly muddled. Reward whoever can explain Q/K/V back using the library analogy in their own words — that's the sign it landed.
slides.marketConnection: Mastering these seven ideas means you can now read any 2026 model card or architecture blog (Llama, DeepSeek, Gemma) and actually understand the 'tweaks' they advertise — they're all variations on these exact components.

keyTakeaways: Self-attention lets every word build its meaning by looking at every other word and weighting their relevance.
Q/K/V is a tiny per-word search engine: Query asks, Key advertises, Value delivers — like searching a library by spine labels then reading contents.
Scaled dot-product attention is four steps: score, scale by sqrt(d_k), softmax into focus weights, blend the values — and you can do it by hand.
Multi-head attention runs several spotlights in parallel so the model captures grammar, reference, and meaning relationships simultaneously.
Positional encoding re-injects word order, because going fully parallel threw the natural order away; residuals + layer norm make deep stacks trainable.
A layer = attention (gather context) + feed-forward (think privately); encoder reads, decoder writes, and most 2026 chat models are decoder-only.
marketSnapshot2026: The architecture dissected today is, with refinements, exactly what runs inside every major 2026 model. The dominant chat assistants (GPT, Gemini, Claude, Llama, Grok) are decoder-only Transformers. The efficiency tweaks making headlines — FlashAttention (faster attention compute), grouped-query attention (cheaper serving), RoPE and long-context extensions (million-token windows), RMSNorm and SwiGLU (better training), and especially Mixture-of-Experts (many specialized feed-forward 'experts' so you scale parameters without scaling cost per token) — are all modifications to the seven components on this slide deck, not replacements for them. When a 2026 release brags about being 'more efficient,' it is almost always optimizing the attention or feed-forward math you just learned to compute by hand.
homework: Take the worked example from slide d2-s4 and compute the new representation for word B (instead of A) by hand, showing all four steps. Then write 3-4 sentences answering: which word's Value dominated B's new representation, and why does that match the Q/K/V intuition? Bring your worked numbers to Day 3.
nextDayTeaser: Tomorrow we answer the question that's been lurking all day: how does a Transformer turn raw text into numbers, and how does it actually learn to talk? We'll cover tokenization (how 'unbelievable' becomes pieces), embeddings, the next-token training objective, loss and softmax, teacher forcing, attention masks, what a 'context window' really means — and the secret sauce of generation: greedy, beam, temperature, top-k and top-p. By the end you'll understand exactly why the same model can sound boringly safe or wildly creative depending on a single dial.
